summary: Jeff Atwood examines the phenomenon of forking in open source software, using the Pidgin/Funpidgin split as a case study. The Pidgin developers made a controversial UI change to the text input field and refused to revert it despite user backlash, leading to the creation of Funpidgin. Atwood outlines the four possible outcomes of any fork: the fork dies, merges back, replaces the original, or both survive independently. He argues that while forking is a fundamental right in open source and embodies 'freedom zero,' it is extremely difficult to execute successfully due to the inertia around established projects.
key_points: Forking differs from simple branching by intent - a true fork aims to replace or compete with the original project
The Pidgin developers' refusal to accommodate user preferences on a UI change sparked a community revolt and subsequent fork
There are exactly four possible outcomes for any fork: death, merger, replacement of original, or coexistence
Established open source projects have tremendous inertia that makes successful forking very difficult
Forks face challenges including divided communities, needing to differentiate, and the original project's ability to absorb their improvements
money_quotes: Whether anyone else in the world will care about their crazy new version of the code is not at all clear, but that's not the point.
What is different about a fork is intent. In a fork, the person(s) creating the fork intend for the fork to replace or compete with the original project they are forking.
The community's response was swift: Oh yeah? Fork you!
Unlike the Pidgin developers, we believe the user should have the final say in what goes into the program.
Forking is incredibly difficult to pull off.
Just as in real evolution, I suspect that most forks die in vast, nameless numbers, before they become strong enough to engender any forked progeny of their own.
Forking is the absolute bedrock of open source software – but it is also not a path to be chosen lightly.
key_insight: Forking is the ultimate expression of open source freedom, but the tremendous inertia around established projects means most forks fail before they ever gain traction.
